question.text: "Fundamental attributes" of a mutual fund scheme include all of the following EXCEPT:

answer.text: The name of the fund manager managing the scheme
The type/category of the mutual fund scheme
The investment objective of the scheme
The asset allocation pattern of the scheme

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Fundamental attributes include the scheme type/category, investment objective, and asset allocation pattern — these define the core character of the scheme. The fund manager's name is NOT a fundamental attribute because fund managers can change without altering the scheme's fundamental nature.
**The Trap:** Candidates often assume that the fund manager is a fundamental attribute because fund managers significantly influence performance. However, a fund manager change does not require the same unitholder protection (written notice + load-free exit) that a change in fundamental attributes does.
**Cross-Exam Context:** NISM V-B and XII test what qualifies as a fundamental attribute because changes to these attributes trigger mandatory investor protection measures (notice + exit option).
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** While fund manager changes are not fundamental attribute changes, they still matter to investors. Proactively inform your clients when fund managers change. A star fund manager leaving can be a signal to review the investment thesis even though it does not trigger the formal fundamental attribute change process.
